claims.claim: Emergency Ordinance detainees exceed 600 under remand
claims.source: No primary source found for a 2025 figure of over 600 detainees under Emergency Ordinance provisions. Contrary context: Ministry of Home Affairs, Crime Prevention Board page, last updated 20 June 2025, states POCA was amended after repeal of the Emergency Ordinance 1969 and ISA 1960: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/index.php/en/maklumat-korporat/badan-eksekutif/lembaga-pencegahan-jenayah
claims.correction: Do not publish the 600 figure unless a primary source such as a parliamentary written answer, Home Ministry statistic, prison/remand statistic, court record, or official detainee list is found. Also clarify whether the claim concerns EO 1969 detainees, POCA/POTA/SOSMA detainees, ordinary remand prisoners, or another category.
claims.stale_check: The claim is dated as of 2025. The key legal change is not recent: the relevant Emergency Proclamations were annulled in 2011 and EO 1969 was treated by official sources as repealed/abolished.

claims.claim: Over 600 individuals remained detained under various Emergency Ordinance provisions as of 2025, despite states of emergency having ended.
claims.source: Malaysia government submission to OHCHR, HRI/CORE/MYS/2016, paras. 49 and footnote 9, states the 1966, 1969 and 1977 Emergency Proclamations were annulled on 20 December 2011 after motions passed by both Houses on 24 November 2011 and 20 December 2011: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D
claims.correction: The part saying the emergency proclamations had ended is verified. The part saying over 600 people remained detained under Emergency Ordinance provisions in 2025 is not verified and is doubtful without an official detainee dataset or parliamentary answer.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that would make a 2025 EO-detainee figure plausible; official sources describe the emergency proclamations as annulled in 2011.

claims.claim: Over 600 people detained under Emergency Ordinances after emergencies ended
claims.source: Malaysia government submission to OHCHR, HRI/CORE/MYS/2016, footnote 9, confirms emergency proclamations were annulled in 2011; no primary source found for over 600 post-annulment EO detainees: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced statement about the 2011 annulment and current preventive-detention laws, or produce the primary source for the 600 detainees.
claims.stale_check: The relevant repeal/annulment dates predate the analysis by many years.

claims.claim: Preventive detention continues under laws that should have lapsed
claims.source: Ministry of Home Affairs, Crime Prevention Board page, last updated 20 June 2025, identifies current implementation of the Prevention of Crime Act 1959 and Prevention of Terrorism Act 2015, not EO detention: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/index.php/en/maklumat-korporat/badan-eksekutif/lembaga-pencegahan-jenayah
claims.correction: Preventive detention continues in Malaysia under statutes such as POCA and POTA, but the analysis does not verify that current detention continues under Emergency Ordinance laws that have lapsed.
claims.stale_check: Not a stale issue; current preventive-detention law has shifted from EO/ISA to POCA/POTA/SOSMA frameworks.

claims.claim: Emergency (Essential Powers) Ordinance 1969 provisions remain in force via savings clause
claims.source: Emergency (Essential Powers) Act 1979, Act 216, sections 9 and 13, official source identified as AGC Laws of Malaysia PDF at https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/Act%20216%20-Original.pdf; accessible mirror text at Refworld: https://www.refworld.org/legal/legislation/natlegbod/1971/14035
claims.correction: Act 216 section 13 repealed the Emergency (Essential Powers) Ordinance 1969. The savings clause preserved subsidiary legislation made under the Ordinance until repealed or replaced; it does not mean all Ordinance provisions themselves remain in force.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found; this legal structure dates to Act 216, gazetted 25 January 1979.

claims.claim: Federal Court has not definitively ruled on validity of post-emergency detentions
claims.source: No primary source found proving the negative proposition. Relevant historical case context includes IGP & Anor v Lee Kim Hoong [1979], discussed in relation to EO detention and Article 150 laying requirements, but that was not a post-2011 detention ruling: https://www.themalaysianinsight.com/s/324326
claims.correction: Avoid asserting absence of a definitive Federal Court ruling unless a complete case-law search is done. If used, narrow it to: no primary source was found in this check for a Federal Court ruling on post-2011 EO detentions.
claims.stale_check: No recent ruling was found in this search, but the absence of a ruling requires more comprehensive legal database verification.

claims.claim: Article 150 emergency powers intended as temporary measures during crises
claims.source: Federal Constitution Article 150 text, hosted at WIPO Lex, states emergency proclamation requires a grave emergency threatening security, economic life or public order, and Article 150(3) provides for laying/annulment by both Houses: https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/ru/legislation/details/13054
claims.correction: This is broadly supported by Article 150, but the phrase 'intended as temporary' is an interpretation. Better: Article 150 empowers emergency action where a grave emergency exists and provides mechanisms for annulment and cessation.

claims.claim: Using emergency laws decades after emergency contradicts constitutional design
claims.source: Federal Constitution Article 150 text, hosted at WIPO Lex: https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/ru/legislation/details/13054; Malaysia government OHCHR submission states annulment ensured Malaysia was no longer under a State of Emergency: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D
claims.correction: This is a legal opinion rather than a directly verifiable fact. Attribute it to a legal expert, court, Bar resolution, or constitutional analysis, and distinguish it from verified statutory facts.

claims.claim: 76% of detainees under EO provisions have been held for over 3 years
claims.source: No primary source found for the 76% figure, the denominator, the detainee population, or the date. Searches did not locate a Home Ministry, Prisons Department, parliamentary answer, court, SUHAKAM, or official detention-board source supporting it.
claims.correction: Remove the 76% figure unless the original primary source is produced. If it refers to a non-EO detention regime, rename the regime and provide the denominator and date.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found; the main issue is lack of primary verification.

claims.claim: Families describe indefinite detention as destroying livelihoods and mental health
claims.source: No primary source found in the supplied analysis. This would require direct interviews, affidavits, court filings, SUHAKAM testimony, or named family statements.
claims.correction: Attribute to named interviews, affidavits, or a specific report, and distinguish anecdotal testimony from verified detention statistics.

claims.claim: 600 people detained under emergency laws with no emergency is normalised injustice
claims.source: Malaysia government OHCHR submission verifies no ongoing state of emergency after annulment of the 1966, 1969 and 1977 proclamations in 2011, but no primary source verifies 600 current EO detainees: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D
claims.correction: The normative phrase can remain only if the factual predicate is verified. At present, the 600-current-EO-detainees claim is unsupported.

claims.claim: Malaysian Bar passed resolution calling for review of all EO detentions in 2024
claims.source: Malaysian Bar, Resolutions Adopted at the 78th AGM, 16 March 2024, lists six resolutions. The relevant resolution calls for repeal of draconian laws and reforms, including POCA, SOSMA, POTA and other laws, but does not call for review of all EO detentions: https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/agms-and-egms/resolutions/resolutions-adopted-at-the-78th-annual-general-meeting-of-the-malaysian-bar-held-at-menara-kembar-bank-rakyat-kuala-lumpur-saturday-16-mar-2024- and PDF https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/cms/upload_files/document/5.%20Resolution%20of%20Growing%20Concern%20over%20Delay%20of%20Government%20to%20Repeal%20Draconian%20Laws%20and%20Bring%20about%20Needed%20Reforms%20to%20Ensure%20Justice%20in%20Malaysia.pdf
claims.correction: Correct to: Malaysian Bar passed 2024 resolutions calling for repeal of draconian laws and addressing SOSMA cases; no verified resolution was found calling for review of all EO detentions.
claims.stale_check: Not applicable; the 2024 Bar resolution text is available and contradicts the claim.

claims.claim: T4A related issue 1988 is the source for this analysis
claims.source: Local analysis JSON supplied by user lists sources as only 'T4A related issue 1988'. This is not a primary source for factual verification.
claims.correction: A previous T4A issue is circular sourcing and should not be treated as factual support. Replace with primary sources.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Over 600 individuals remained detained under various Emergency Ordinance provisions as of 2025
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The specific number, detention regime, and date could not be traced to a primary source. Official sources instead emphasize repeal/annulment of the emergency framework.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Malaysia OHCHR submission HRI/CORE/MYS/2016, paras. 49 and footnote 9: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 76% of detainees under EO provisions have been held for over 3 years
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source found for the 76% figure, the base population, the dates, or the legal category.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Emergency (Essential Powers) Ordinance 1969 provisions remain in force via savings clause
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Act 216 section 13 repealed the Ordinance and saved subsidiary legislation made under it; the analysis overstates this as the Ordinance provisions themselves remaining in force.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Emergency (Essential Powers) Act 1979, Act 216, sections 9 and 13, AGC Laws of Malaysia PDF source: https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/Act%20216%20-Original.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysian Bar passed resolution calling for review of all EO detentions in 2024
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The Malaysian Bar 2024 AGM resolution list and relevant PDFs do not show a resolution calling for review of all EO detentions.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Preventive detention continues under laws that should have lapsed
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Current preventive detention may continue under POCA/POTA/SOSMA, but the analysis frames this as current EO detention without proving current EO detention. That conflates distinct legal regimes.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Ministry of Home Affairs, Crime Prevention Board page, last updated 20 June 2025: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/index.php/en/maklumat-korporat/badan-eksekutif/lembaga-pencegahan-jenayah

omitted_facts: Malaysia's own OHCHR core document says the 1966, 1969 and 1977 Emergency Proclamations were annulled on 20 December 2011, with motions passed in the Dewan Rakyat on 24 November 2011 and Dewan Negara on 20 December 2011: https://docstore.ohchr.org/SelfServices/FilesHandler.ashx?enc=kXbwfzyovcC%2BJYGJ0achMGvenT0Nj328fs3SeY8x1EqPLKU1YyRHuCxeeTxYPJkFs5KDB8gumKHYIUYWMKue7w%3D%3D

The Ministry of Home Affairs describes the Prevention of Crime Act 1959 and Prevention of Terrorism Act 2015 as the current laws administered by the relevant board, and says POCA was amended after repeal of the Emergency Ordinance 1969 and ISA 1960: https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/index.php/en/maklumat-korporat/badan-eksekutif/lembaga-pencegahan-jenayah

The Emergency (Essential Powers) Act 1979 section 13 repealed the Emergency (Essential Powers) Ordinance 1969 but saved subsidiary legislation; this is narrower than saying the Ordinance provisions remain in force: https://www.refworld.org/legal/legislation/natlegbod/1971/14035

The Malaysian Bar's 2024 AGM resolutions include calls for repeal of draconian laws and a separate SOSMA resolution, but not a verified resolution calling for review of all EO detentions: https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/article/news/agms-and-egms/resolutions/resolutions-adopted-at-the-78th-annual-general-meeting-of-the-malaysian-bar-held-at-menara-kembar-bank-rakyat-kuala-lumpur-saturday-16-mar-2024-
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only one source, 'T4A related issue 1988', which is circular and not a primary source. It does not cite government detainee statistics, parliamentary answers, prison/remand records, court judgments, Malaysian Bar resolution PDFs, SUHAKAM records, or family testimony. The source base has no demonstrated political or institutional diversity and omits government, judicial, legal-professional, human-rights, and affected-family primary evidence.
overall_assessment: The analysis is not reliable for publication in its current form. Some background propositions are partly supported, especially that Malaysia's older emergency proclamations were annulled in 2011 and Article 150 is an emergency-power provision. However, the core reader-facing claims about over 600 current EO detainees, 76% held over three years, and a 2024 Malaysian Bar resolution reviewing EO detentions are unverified or contradicted by the available primary material. The issue should be rewritten around verified current preventive-detention regimes or held until primary detainee data is obtained.
